narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your high family income background, solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which is above the typical student's 3.21—and a strong LSAT score of 37, just edging out the average of 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated an 80.3% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signals underperformance relative to peers compared to the average Year 3 decile of 5.53. Your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern strongly, being much lower than the typical 5.71 and highlighting early struggles that weighed heavily against you. On a brighter note, your high family income helped pull the prediction toward success, countering some of the risk, while your above-average undergrad GPA and LSAT score also nudged it positively, though not enough to overcome the top concerns. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and mid-school deciles stand out as notably lower, which the model sees as the strongest signals of bar exam risk in cases like yours. While your personal background as a Black woman brings unique resilience and perspective to the field, the model focused strictly on these performance metrics. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions—perhaps seek targeted academic support, additional test prep, or even part-time options if it fits your goals. You're clearly capable in key areas, and with focused effort on grades, you can reapply stronger. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
